title: Prevent 'system migrate' from touching all containers
body:
### Feature request description

This originates from this Toolbx issue: https://github.com/containers/toolbox/issues/1621

The `toolbox(1)` binary calls `podman system migrate` at start-up when it detects a new Podman version, and it does that only once after an upgrade.  It started doing this long ago to handle changes to rootless containers in Podman 1.3.0.

The problem is that the `system migrate` command migrates all containers.  If there are running non-Toolbx containers, then it will stop them, migrate them, and restart them.  This seems to be surprising for users who are left wondering why using `toolbox(1)` restarted their other containers.

It might be good if there was a way to only migrate a subset of containers.

### Suggest potential solution

One possibility could be:
```ShellSession
$ podman system migrate --filter "label=com.github.containers.toolbox=true"
```

### Have you considered any alternatives?

Maybe `system migrate` hasn't been needed in a long time, and `toolbox(1)` should stop using it?  Although, having the mechanism in place does offer a smooth way to handle future backwards compatibility issues.

### Additional context

I am a bit surprised that this is the first time I got a report about this, because Toolbx has been doing it since May 2019.

The report came from a Fedora CoreOS user, where there's a much bigger likelihood of having non-Toolbx containers, compared to Fedora Silverblue or Workstation, which have historically seen the lion's share of Toolbx use.

comments.body:
I think the main reason is that system migrate was designed to migrate things once. It certainly has not been needed in years so calling it all the time is a bit odd.

One reason why it is a all or nothing is that system migrate is documented to reset the entire user namespace, i.e. it will stop all containers to then kill the pause process and thus the user namespace. This is so the next command would create a new use namespace with (potentially) new uid/gid mappings from /etc/subuid /etc/subgid. 

So in this context migrating a single container makes no real sense. Calling `podman system migrate` (without --new-runtime) on a container would do absolutely nothing these days it has one logic to migrate a conmon pid file path which was done for podman v1.3 but this is really the only thing so I would say this command is really not needed.

And from a UX perspective it is a failure to require user to run this command post update. We avoid breaking changes to the container config so migration should generally not be needed. The last time I has to "break" the config was for podman 4.0 https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/0136a66a83d027049da6338f8ce6dfa8052c8ca3 and something I did there was perform a transparent migration on the container the first time we access it which IMO should be how thing are done going forward.

I agree, `podman system migrate` needs to recreate the user namespace, so containers must be restarted, so we can't filter on specific containers.

I'd suggest to drop it from toolbx, it should not affect other containers.

Okay, great!  I will stop calling `podman system migrate`.

Is `podman system migrate --new-runtime` still relevant today?  Toolbx started using it during the runc to crun transition for cgroups v2, if `podman start` fails with:
```
ERRO[0000]: oci runtime "runc" does not support CGroups V2: use
        system migrate to mitigate
```

I mean the code likely still works to switch runtimes but I don't think it is needed for anything currently.
runc supports cgroupv2 for a while now so there should be no need to switch for that.

> I don't think it is needed for anything currently.

Okay, thanks!
